metadata.summary: Multi-platform pixel + server-side (CAPI) setup to track macro/micro conversions, bypass browser restrictions (ad blockers, iOS privacy), and feed accurate signal to smart bidding.

content.title: Ads Conversion Tracking
content.summary: Multi-platform conversion tracking setup: define macro and micro conversion events with dollar values, install browser-side pixels (Meta Pixel, Google Tag, LinkedIn Insight Tag), implement server-side APIs (Meta CAPI, Google Ads offline upload), verify all events fire correctly, and configure attribution windows. The core rule is: always implement server-side (CAPI/Conversions API) in addition to browser pixels — browser tracking degrades by 20-40% due to ad blockers and iOS privacy restrictions, causing smart bidding to optimize on incomplete data.

why.p: Ad platform algorithms (Meta, Google) optimize bidding toward the conversion signal you provide. If browser tracking is the only source and 30% of events are blocked, the algorithm sees inflated CPA and under-delivers on good audiences. Server-side events pass directly from your server, bypassing browser restrictions, and can include offline CRM data. Accurate conversion data is the single highest-leverage optimization lever in paid advertising.

list.item: Setting up conversion tracking on a new site, app, or ad account
Auditing tracking accuracy when reported conversions don't match CRM data
Implementing Meta Conversions API (CAPI) for server-side event deduplication
Uploading offline conversions from CRM systems to Meta or Google
Configuring attribution windows to match the product's sales cycle length
Attribution model comparison and selection — use ads-attribution-models
Analytics property setup (GA4 events, Mixpanel) — this covers ad-platform pixels only
Campaign creation — tracking must be in place first; see ads-meta-campaign-setup
Bid strategy configuration — tracking is a prerequisite, not the bidding logic itself

section.name: conversion-definition
section.p: Conversion tracking starts with taxonomy: macro conversions are primary business goals, while micro conversions are funnel steps that provide signal during ramp-up when macro volume is low.

rule.statement: Always track both macro conversions (primary business goal) and micro conversions (funnel steps) — micro conversions provide optimization signal when macro volume is too low for smart bidding.
rule.rationale: Smart bidding requires 30-50 conversions per week to function; new campaigns rarely achieve this on macro events alone. Micro conversions (add-to-cart, pricing page view) fill the signal gap during ramp-up.

list.item: Macro — Purchase, Trial start, Lead form submit, Account registration
Micro — Add to cart, Begin checkout, Pricing page view, Content download

rule.statement: Pass dynamic purchase values whenever possible — static values prevent Target ROAS bidding from optimizing for high-value orders over low-value ones.
rule.rationale: Static values make all conversions look equal to the algorithm; with dynamic values, bidding can allocate more spend toward user segments that produce larger orders.

list.item: Fixed value — Lead = $50 (estimated average value)
Dynamic value — Purchase = actual transaction amount (recommended)
Estimated LTV value — Trial = $100 (based on historical LTV/conversion rate)

section.p: Cross-platform event name mapping ensures consistency across Meta, Google, and LinkedIn:

list.item: Sign up: Meta=CompleteRegistration, Google=sign_up, LinkedIn=signUp
Lead: Meta=Lead, Google=generate_lead, LinkedIn=lead
Add to cart: Meta=AddToCart, Google=add_to_cart, LinkedIn=N/A
Purchase: Meta=Purchase, Google=purchase, LinkedIn=conversion
Trial: Meta=StartTrial, Google=trial_started, LinkedIn=N/A

section.name: pixel-installation
section.p: Browser-side pixels must be installed on every page to enable audience building and retargeting across the site, not just on conversion pages.

rule.statement: Install the Meta Pixel base code on every page of the site, not just conversion pages.
rule.rationale: The base code enables audience building from all page visitors, not just converters — required for website custom audiences and retargeting.

section.reference: Meta Pixel base code with PageView

example.title: Meta standard event calls
Google Ads conversion event
example.note: transaction_id deduplicates conversions if the thank-you page fires twice — always include it for purchase events.

section.name: server-side-tracking
section.p: Server-side tracking via Meta CAPI and Google Conversions API bypasses all browser restrictions and provides the ground truth for optimization.

rule.statement: Always implement server-side tracking alongside browser pixels — never rely on browser pixels alone.
rule.rationale: Ad blockers, iOS App Tracking Transparency, and browser privacy features block 20-40% of browser pixel events. Server-side events pass directly from your server, bypassing all browser restrictions, and can include richer user data for better match rates.

example.title: Meta CAPI Node.js implementation
example.note: event_id must match the browser pixel's eventID parameter to enable Meta's deduplication — same event_id from browser + server = counted once.

rule.statement: Set matching event_id values in both the browser fbq() call and the CAPI server event — mismatched IDs cause double-counting, inflating reported conversions by up to 2x.
rule.rationale: Meta deduplicates events within a 48-hour window when event_id matches; without this, the algorithm sees twice the conversions and bids accordingly, wasting budget.

section.name: verification
section.p: Verification tools and testing processes vary by platform but follow the same principle: confirm events fire in production before and after launch.

list.item: Meta: Meta Pixel Helper (Chrome extension) + Events Manager Test Events tab
Google: Google Tag Assistant (Chrome extension) + Google Ads Tag Diagnostics
LinkedIn: LinkedIn Insight Tag Helper (Chrome extension)
All platforms: Browser DevTools → Network tab → filter by pixel domain

rule.statement: Test every conversion type manually before launch — never rely on code review alone to confirm events fire in production.
rule.rationale: CSP headers, async loading conflicts, and SPA routing all cause pixel silencing in production that is invisible in staging environments.

list.item: Install browser verification extension
Complete a test conversion action (form submit, simulated purchase)
Confirm event appears in platform's real-time event viewer
Verify the value field contains the correct amount and currency
If CAPI implemented: confirm server event appears separately from browser event
Monitor Events Manager for 24-48 hours post-launch for error rates

rule.statement: Set attribution windows to match your product's typical sales cycle — a B2B SaaS with a 30-day trial should use a 30-day click window, not 7 days.
rule.rationale: Shorter windows than the sales cycle under-attribute conversions to ads, making campaigns appear less efficient and causing premature budget cuts on campaigns that are actually working.

list.item: Meta default: 7-day click, 1-day view
Google default: 30-day click
LinkedIn default: 30-day click, 7-day view

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: Tracking work has two phases: build (one-time) and monitor (continuous). Build phase requires high human-in-loop oversight; monitor phase is autonomous.

Build phase: agent generates pixel snippets, infra agent deploys them, QA agent runs synthetic conversions and verifies. Monitor phase: agent diffs Meta Events Manager numbers vs GA4 vs server logs nightly, alerts on >10% delta. Never let an agent silently re-deploy pixel code without seeing a live Test Event — broken tracking is invisible until the campaign report.

list.item: faion-ads-agent — generates pixel/CAPI code per platform, configures conversion actions in ad platforms.
improver — nightly reconciliation: pixel vs server vs GA4, drift alerts.
faion-sdd-executor-agent — gates production deploy of new tracking behind synthetic tests.

example.title: Pixel generation prompt
example.code: Generate pixel + CAPI for {platform=Meta} on {framework=Next.js}. Events: PageView (auto), ViewContent (product page), AddToCart, InitiateCheckout, Purchase. Use event_id = order_id for dedup. Hash email/phone with SHA-256 before sending. Output: client snippet, server endpoint, env-var list.

example.title: Tracking reconciliation prompt
example.code: Reconcile {date_range}: pull Meta Insights conversions, GA4 events, Stripe payments. Group by date. Report rows where |meta - stripe| / stripe > 0.15. For each, list missing event_ids and propose fix (CAPI gap vs pixel block).

rule.statement: Always send a unique event_id (order ID, lead ID) on both pixel and CAPI for the same event — Meta dedupes within a 48-hour window. No event_id = double-count.
rule.rationale: Event ID is the primary deduplication key. Without it, the same business event gets counted twice, inflating conversion reports and burning budget.

rule.statement: Hash PII (email, phone, first/last name, city, zip) with lowercase-trim then SHA-256 before sending — and verify in Events Manager that match-quality score is > 5.0.
rule.rationale: PII hashing enables Meta to match server events to users without transmitting PII in plaintext. Match quality score measures success; scores below 5.0 indicate data quality issues.

rule.statement: Fire one and only one Purchase per order. If your thank-you page can be reloaded, gate the pixel on sessionStorage or send only from server.
rule.rationale: Multiple Purchase events per order inflate reported conversions and make optimization signal noisy. Server-side de-duplication is the most reliable approach.

rule.statement: Keep event names canonical across platforms via a single mapping table; never sprinkle platform-specific names through the codebase.
rule.rationale: Event name standardization prevents drift where the same business action gets different names on different platforms. A mapping table makes audits and migrations easier.

rule.statement: Set CAPI as the source of truth in the ad platform once both are stable — pixel becomes a redundant backup, not the primary signal.
rule.rationale: CAPI provides server-side data that is more accurate and less subject to browser restrictions. Once CAPI is working, make it primary so algorithms optimize on the most reliable signal.

rule.statement: Test on an iOS Safari device with Private Relay on — that's where 30% of the data loss hides.
rule.rationale: iOS Private Relay removes IP addresses from server-side requests for some users. Testing with Private Relay reveals fingerprinting gaps and match-rate drops that staging environments miss.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Tracking has hard limits due to platform architecture and privacy constraints that no implementation can overcome.

antipattern.description: iOS 14.5+ ATT, Safari ITP, and EU consent banners cut browser-only pixel coverage by 30-60% — server-side is mandatory for accurate numbers.
antipattern.reason: Browser privacy features are beyond an advertiser's control. Browser-only tracking is inherently incomplete in 2026; CAPI is the only path to ground truth.

antipattern.description: Meta CAPI events without event_id get deduped against the pixel by event_name + timestamp window — easy to double-count if you skip event IDs.
antipattern.reason: Meta's fallback deduplication is imprecise. Events with the same name fired within seconds can appear different. Event IDs prevent this false-positive dedup.

antipattern.description: predicted_ltv on Meta is a custom param Meta doesn't actually use for bidding (yet) — don't optimize on it.
antipattern.reason: Meta's documentation promises LTV-aware bidding but has not shipped it. Agents that instrument predicted_ltv are building for a future that may not arrive; focus on actual conversion signals instead.

antipattern.description: LinkedIn Insight Tag has 90-day attribution but only fires on direct site visits — single-page apps need explicit _linkedin_partner_id push on route change.
antipattern.reason: LinkedIn's tag is not universal; SPAs must hook route changes manually. Agents deploying LinkedIn without SPA-specific handling will get zero conversions.

antipattern.description: gtag/GTM server containers don't replicate Meta's automatic advanced matching — you must hash and send PII fields explicitly.
antipattern.reason: Advanced matching requires PII; GTM's server container defaults to anonymous tracking. Agents must explicitly include PII hashing to enable matching.

antipattern.description: Apple's Private Relay strips IP from server-side requests for some users — fingerprinting fallbacks degrade silently.
antipattern.reason: When IP is unavailable, match rates drop sharply. Agents are blind to the failure because no error is raised — the event lands but with zero match rate.

template.purpose: Conversion tracking plan: macro/micro events, values, platforms, implementation checklist
Meta Pixel base code snippet with PageView
